items.title: Great Expectations
items.text: A Python data validation framework. Test files leave CheckpointResult or ExpectationSuiteValidationResult objects in module globals. The runner emits one event per ExpectationValidationResult. Scrutin auto-detects Great Expectations when a tests/great_expectations/ directory is present.

items.title: Directory structure
items.text:
myproject/
├── pyproject.toml
├── src/
│   └── myproject/
│       └── __init__.py
└── tests/
    └── great_expectations/
        └── test_orders.py

items.title: Minimal example
items.text:
tests/great_expectations/test_orders.py

import great_expectations as gx
import great_expectations.expectations as gxe
import pandas as pd

suite = gx.ExpectationSuite(name="orders_suite")
suite.add_expectation(gxe.ExpectColumnValuesToNotBeNull(column="id"))
suite.add_expectation(
    gxe.ExpectColumnValuesToBeBetween(column="value", min_value=0, max_value=100)
)

context = gx.get_context(mode="ephemeral")
source = context.data_sources.add_pandas("orders")
asset = source.add_dataframe_asset(name="orders")
batch_def = asset.add_batch_definition_whole_dataframe("batch")

df = pd.DataFrame({"id": [1, 2, 3], "value": [10, 20, 30]})
batch = batch_def.get_batch(batch_parameters={"dataframe": df})
result = batch.validate(suite)

Every top-level ExpectationSuiteValidationResult or CheckpointResult left in module globals after execution is picked up by the runner.

items.title: Running
items.text:
scrutin myproject              # TUI
scrutin -r plain myproject     # text output

items.title: Configuration
items.text:
No configuration is required. To override defaults in scrutin.toml:

[[suite]]
tool        = "great_expectations"
test_dirs = ["tests/great_expectations"]
source_dirs = ["src"]

items.title: Dependencies
items.text: Great Expectations and pandas must be installed in the active virtual environment. If the import fails, the runner emits a single error event for the file; other suites are unaffected.

items.title: jarl
items.text: An R linter that scrutin can drive as a plugin. Unlike test tools, jarl checks code style rather than correctness. It maps lint diagnostics to warn events, so lint issues appear alongside test results in the TUI and web dashboard. Scrutin auto-detects jarl when a jarl.toml file, a DESCRIPTION file, and an R/ directory are all present.

items.title: Directory structure
items.text:
mypackage/
├── DESCRIPTION
├── jarl.toml
└── R/
    ├── math.R
    └── strings.R

items.title: Minimal example
items.text:
jarl.toml

An empty file is enough to opt in. jarl uses its built-in rule set by default.

R/math.R

# jarl flags T/F instead of TRUE/FALSE
is_positive <- function(x) {
  if (x > 0) T else F
}

items.title: Running
items.text:
scrutin mypackage              # TUI
scrutin -r plain mypackage     # text output

jarl runs concurrently alongside any test tools in the same project.

items.title: Plugin actions
items.text:
Press a in the TUI (or the action button in the web) to open the action palette. jarl defines two entries, both of which run across the whole suite:

Action Description Jarl: fix all Apply safe fixes to every file in the suite Jarl: fix all (unsafe) Apply safe + unsafe fixes to every file in the suite

Both invoke jarl once with every matching file (after include / exclude filters) as trailing arguments. After a fix, the affected files are re-linted automatically.

items.title: Configuration
items.text:
No configuration is required beyond the jarl.toml marker file. To override defaults in scrutin.toml:

[[suite]]
tool        = "jarl"
test_dirs = ["R"]

jarl has no source directories (it lints the files directly, it does not track dependencies).

items.title: pointblank
items.text: A data validation framework for R. Test files are .R scripts that produce interrogated ptblank_agent objects. The runner emits one event per validation step. Scrutin auto-detects pointblank when a DESCRIPTION file and a tests/pointblank/ directory are both present.

items.title: Directory structure
items.text:
mypackage/
├── DESCRIPTION
├── R/
│   └── helpers.R
└── tests/
    └── pointblank/
        └── test_users.R

items.title: Minimal example
items.text:
tests/pointblank/test_users.R

users <- data.frame(
  id    = 1:5,
  email = c("a@x.com", "b@x.com", "c@x.com", "d@x.com", "e@x.com"),
  age   = c(22, 31, 45, 19, 28)
)

agent <- pointblank::create_agent(tbl = users, tbl_name = "users") |>
  pointblank::col_vals_not_null(columns = "id") |>
  pointblank::col_vals_not_null(columns = "email") |>
  pointblank::col_vals_between(columns = "age", left = 18, right = 65) |>
  pointblank::interrogate()

Every top-level ptblank_agent object left in the environment after sourcing is picked up by the runner. You can have multiple agents per file.

items.title: Running
items.text:
scrutin mypackage              # TUI
scrutin -r plain mypackage     # text output

items.title: Configuration
items.text:
No configuration is required. To override defaults in scrutin.toml:

[[suite]]
tool        = "pointblank"
test_dirs = ["tests/pointblank"]
source_dirs = ["R"]

items.title: pytest
items.text: The standard testing framework for Python. Scrutin auto-detects pytest when a pyproject.toml (or setup.py/setup.cfg) is present alongside a tests/ or test/ directory, or test_*.py files at the project root.

items.title: Directory structure
items.text:
myproject/
├── pyproject.toml
├── src/
│   └── myproject/
│       ├── __init__.py
│       └── math.py
└── tests/
    └── test_math.py

items.title: Minimal example
items.text:
src/myproject/math.py

def add(x: int, y: int) -> int:
    return x + y

tests/test_math.py

from myproject import add

def test_add():
    assert add(2, 3) == 5
    assert add(-1, 1) == 0

items.title: Running
items.text:
scrutin myproject              # TUI
scrutin -r plain myproject     # text output

items.title: Configuration
items.text:
No configuration is required. To override defaults in scrutin.toml:

[[suite]]
tool        = "pytest"
test_dirs = ["tests"]
source_dirs = ["src"]

items.title: Virtual environment
items.text:
Scrutin auto-detects your Python virtual environment. Detection order:

  1. [python].interpreter in scrutin.toml
  2. [python].venv in scrutin.toml
  3. $VIRTUAL_ENV environment variable
  4. .venv/ or venv/ in the project root
  5. $CONDA_PREFIX environment variable
  6. python3 on $PATH (or python on Windows)

Override in config:

[python]
venv = ".venv"

items.title: Extra pytest flags
items.text:
Pass arbitrary flags through to pytest.main() via [pytest] extra_args. Appended verbatim to every invocation, letting you reach for obscure pytest knobs without scrutin growing a CLI option for each one:

[pytest]
extra_args = ["--tb=short", "-vv"]

items.title: Custom runner
items.text:
scrutin init writes the default runner to .scrutin/pytest/runner.py. Point to your edited copy either globally or on the specific [[suite]]:

[pytest]
runner = ".scrutin/pytest/runner.py"

or

[[suite]]
tool        = "pytest"
test_dirs = ["tests"]
runner    = ".scrutin/pytest/runner.py"

items.title: ruff
items.text: A fast Python linter written in Rust. Like jarl for R, ruff is not a test framework: lint diagnostics map to warn events, clean files produce a synthetic pass, and lint issues appear alongside test results in the TUI and web dashboard. Scrutin auto-detects ruff when a ruff configuration marker is present (ruff.toml, .ruff.toml, or a [tool.ruff] section in pyproject.toml).

items.title: Directory structure
items.text:
myproject/
├── pyproject.toml
└── src/
    └── myproject/
        ├── __init__.py
        └── utils.py

items.title: Minimal example
items.text:
pyproject.toml

[project]
name = "myproject"
version = "0.1.0"

[tool.ruff]
line-length = 88

The [tool.ruff] section is enough to opt in. Alternatively, place a ruff.toml or .ruff.toml at the project root.

src/myproject/utils.py

import os, sys  # E401: multiple imports on one line

def greet(name):
    x = 42  # F841: unused variable
    return f"hello, {name}"

items.title: Running
items.text:
scrutin myproject              # TUI
scrutin -r plain myproject     # text output

ruff runs concurrently alongside any test tools in the same project. It uses command mode (calling ruff check --output-format json directly), so no Python subprocess is needed.

items.title: Plugin actions
items.text:
Press a in the TUI (or the action button in the web) to open the action palette. ruff defines two entries, both of which run across the whole suite:

Action Description Ruff: fix all Apply safe fixes to every file in the suite Ruff: fix all (unsafe) Apply safe + unsafe fixes to every file in the suite

Both invoke ruff check --fix once with every matching file (after include / exclude filters) as trailing arguments. After a fix, the affected files are re-linted automatically.

items.title: Configuration
items.text:
No scrutin-specific configuration is required beyond the ruff config marker. ruff's own configuration (ruff.toml, .ruff.toml, or [tool.ruff] in pyproject.toml) controls which rules are enabled, excluded paths, and other linter settings.

To override the default suite in scrutin.toml:

[[suite]]
tool        = "ruff"
test_dirs = ["."]

ruff lints from the project root by default. It has no source directories (each file is checked independently, no dependency tracking).

items.title: testthat
items.text: The standard testing framework for R packages. Scrutin auto-detects testthat when a DESCRIPTION file and a tests/testthat/ directory are both present.

items.title: Directory structure
items.text:
mypackage/
├── DESCRIPTION
├── NAMESPACE
├── R/
│   └── math.R
└── tests/
    └── testthat/
        └── test-math.R

items.title: Minimal example
items.text:
R/math.R

#' @export
add <- function(x, y) x + y

tests/testthat/test-math.R

test_that("add works", {
  expect_equal(add(2, 3), 5)
  expect_equal(add(-1, 1), 0)
})

items.title: Running
items.text:
scrutin mypackage              # TUI
scrutin -r plain mypackage     # text output

items.title: Configuration
items.text:
No configuration is required. To override defaults in scrutin.toml:

[[suite]]
tool        = "testthat"
test_dirs = ["tests/testthat"]
source_dirs = ["R"]

items.title: Working directory
items.text:
Scrutin runs workers from the project root, not from tests/testthat/. Use testthat::test_path() to build paths to fixture files:

test_that("reads fixture data", {
  d <- read.csv(test_path("fixtures", "data.csv"))
  expect_equal(nrow(d), 10)
})

Bare relative paths like "fixtures/data.csv" will not resolve correctly.

items.title: Package loading
items.text: Workers call pkgload::load_all() to load the package. When a source file changes in watch mode, the engine reloads the package automatically before running affected tests. If you add or change @export or @importFrom tags, run devtools::document() separately: scrutin does not invoke roxygen.

items.title: Custom runner
items.text:
scrutin init writes the default runner to .scrutin/testthat/runner.R. To use an edited copy, point to it either globally (applies to every testthat suite) or on the specific [[suite]]:

[testthat]
runner = ".scrutin/testthat/runner.R"

or

[[suite]]
tool        = "testthat"
test_dirs = ["tests/testthat"]
runner    = ".scrutin/testthat/runner.R"

items.title: tinytest
items.text: A lightweight, zero-dependency testing framework for R packages. Scrutin auto-detects tinytest when a DESCRIPTION file and an inst/tinytest/ directory are both present.

items.title: Directory structure
items.text:
mypackage/
├── DESCRIPTION
├── NAMESPACE
├── R/
│   └── math.R
└── inst/
    └── tinytest/
        └── test_math.R

items.title: Minimal example
items.text:
R/math.R

#' @export
add <- function(x, y) x + y

inst/tinytest/test_math.R

expect_equal(add(2, 3), 5)
expect_equal(add(-1, 1), 0)

tinytest tests are plain R scripts with expect_* calls at the top level (no test_that() wrapper).

items.title: Running
items.text:
scrutin mypackage              # TUI
scrutin -r plain mypackage     # text output

items.title: Configuration
items.text:
No configuration is required. To override defaults in scrutin.toml:

[[suite]]
tool        = "tinytest"
test_dirs = ["inst/tinytest"]
source_dirs = ["R"]

items.title: Custom runner
items.text:
scrutin init writes the default runner to .scrutin/tinytest/runner.R. Point to your edited copy either globally or on the specific [[suite]]:

[tinytest]
runner = ".scrutin/tinytest/runner.R"

or

[[suite]]
tool        = "tinytest"
test_dirs = ["inst/tinytest"]
runner    = ".scrutin/tinytest/runner.R"

items.title: validate
items.text: A rule-based data validation framework for R. Test files are .R scripts that confront() data against a validator() and leave validation objects in the environment. The runner emits one event per validation rule with per-row metrics. Scrutin auto-detects validate when a DESCRIPTION file and a tests/validate/ directory are both present.

items.title: Directory structure
items.text:
mypackage/
├── DESCRIPTION
├── R/
│   └── helpers.R
└── tests/
    └── validate/
        └── test_cars.R

items.title: Minimal example
items.text:
tests/validate/test_cars.R

library(validate)

v <- validator(
  speed_pos   = speed > 0,
  dist_pos    = dist > 0,
  speed_limit = speed < 25
)
result <- confront(cars, v)

Every top-level validation object left in the environment after sourcing is picked up by the runner. Each rule becomes a separate event.

items.title: Running
items.text:
scrutin mypackage              # TUI
scrutin -r plain mypackage     # text output

items.title: Configuration
items.text:
No configuration is required. To override defaults in scrutin.toml:

[[suite]]
tool        = "validate"
test_dirs = ["tests/validate"]
source_dirs = ["R"]
